pests.name: Leaf miner
pests.impact: Adult female flies puncture leaves for sap feeding and egg-laying, causing white spots and leaf drying due to water evaporation. Larvae feed inside leaves, creating thin white tunnels, which reduces photosynthesis. Wounded leaves are susceptible to secondary infections, especially late blight.
pests.solution: Regular field inspection. Utilize natural enemies (Diglyphas isaea, Hemiptersenus semiabiclavus, Opius spp) by breeding them in wooden cages or on white polythene sheets. Use yellow sticky traps. Use butterfly nets in the morning when leaf miners are active. Install a 5-foot high net around the field before cultivation. Deep plowing to destroy weeds and pupae. Remove old infected leaves and bury damaged leaves. Avoid selecting infected seedlings from nurseries. For chemical control, use recommended insecticides: Neem extract (640g/16L), Azadiractin 1% EC (16ml/16L), Abamectin 18g/L EC (9.6ml/16L).

pests.name: Potato tuber moth
pests.impact: Field damage: Larvae tunnel inside leaves, destroying foliage, killing shoots, and causing stems to collapse. Adult females lay eggs on exposed tubers in the soil, damaging them. Storage damage: Adult females lay eggs near tuber eyes; emerging larvae create irregular tunnels inside or near the surface of tubers, destroying quality. Frass from larvae is visible outside the tunnels.
pests.solution: Field control: Regular inspection, use high-quality, uninfected seed tubers, crop rotation to remove host plants, deep planting (approx. 15cm) and good earthing up, good irrigation to seal soil cracks and prevent egg-laying, remove host weeds and maintain field sanitation, timely harvest. If damage is severe, apply Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG (5g/16L). Storage control: Cover stores to prevent adult entry, clean stores and crates before storage. Treat cleaned stores with Pirimiphos methyl 500g/L EC (64ml/16L), Acetamiprid 200g/L SP (16g/16L), or Novaluron 100g/L EC (16ml/16L). Store healthy, undamaged, soil-free tubers. Treat seed tubers before storage with Thiocyclam (Hydrogen Oxalate) 50%SP (40g/100kg). Use pheromone traps for continuous monitoring.

pests.name: Cutworm
pests.impact: Larvae are most active at night, cutting young plant stems at the base. In mature plants, they eat leaves and branches. Damage is more prevalent in dry weather. During tuber development, high larval populations in the soil can eat tubers, reducing market value.
pests.solution: Deep plowing exposes larvae and pupae to the soil surface, making them vulnerable to predatory birds and sunlight. Maintain good weed control and field sanitation in and around the field. Regularly inspect the field and hand-pick larvae hidden in the soil when populations are low. If damage is severe, apply recommended insecticides immediately after planting: Profenophos 500g/LEC (32mL/16L), Etofenprox 100g/LEC (24mL/16L), Chlorfluazuron 50g/LEC (16mL/16L).

pests.name: Golden cyst nematode
pests.impact: No symptoms at low nematode populations. At high populations, symptoms include yellowing of plant leaves, wilting during high environmental temperatures, stunted plant growth, and dwarf plants. When an infected plant is carefully uprooted without damaging the roots, golden-colored nematode cysts can be seen embedded in the roots.
pests.solution: Prevention for uninfected fields: Use certified seed potatoes, avoid using equipment from infected fields, prevent entry of animals and water from nearby infected fields, conduct soil testing before cultivation, practice crop rotation. For mildly infected fields: If cyst count is less than 50 per soil test, practice crop rotation. If cyst count is more than 50, fallow the field for 2-3 years. Avoid cultivating potatoes or other Solanaceae crops for at least 3 seasons. Conduct soil testing before cultivation. For heavily infected fields: Immediately after harvest, treat the field with chlorine powder (70kg/ha) mixed well into the soil, followed by thorough irrigation. Fence off the field to separate it from other cultivated areas. Thoroughly wash all used equipment with a chlorine solution. For infected seeds: Remove all soil from seeds, soak in 1% chlorine solution for two hours, rinse well with water, dry, and then store. Monitor the crop after planting.

pests.name: Aphid
pests.impact: Acts as a virus vector. Sap sucking causes leaf curling, stunted growth, and deformed apices. Excretion of sugary honeydew leads to sooty mold growth on leaves, which hinders the plant's food production process.
pests.solution: Monitor the crop from the beginning using yellow-colored water traps placed in the field. Control weeds to remove host plants. Protect natural enemies, as many are found in the environment. If the population exceeds 20 aphids per hundred leaves, apply recommended insecticides: Thiamethoxam 25% WG (5g/16L), Imidacloprid 70%WG (2g/16L), Thiocyclam (Hydrogen Oxalate) 50% SP (40g/16L), Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG (5g/16L), Buprofezin 25% SC (45ml/16L), Azadirachtine 5%EC (16ml/16L).

pests.name: Thrips
pests.impact: Thrips, equipped with sap-sucking mouthparts, also act as virus vectors. They cause shiny, scratch-like marks on leaves.
pests.solution: Control weeds to remove host plants. Protect natural enemies by avoiding unnecessary insecticide applications. Practice crop rotation. If the population is high, apply recommended insecticides: Thiamethoxam 25% WG (5g/16L), Imidacloprid 70%WG (2g/16L), Thiocyclam (Hydrogen Oxalate) 50% SP (40g/16L), Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG (5g/16L), Buprofezin 25% SC (45ml/16L), Azadirachtine 5%EC (16ml/16L).

pests.name: Whitefly
pests.impact: Whiteflies suck sap from the undersides of leaves. They also act as virus vectors. The honeydew they excrete leads to sooty mold growth on leaves, which hinders the plant's food production process.
pests.solution: Integrated pest management is crucial due to their rapid adaptation to insecticides. Use blue or yellow sticky traps. Control weeds to remove host plants. Use sprinkler irrigation. Rotate different recommended insecticides with varying modes of action: Thiocyclam (Hydrogen Oxalate) 50% SP (40g/16L), Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG (5g/16L), Buprofezin 25% SC (45ml/16L), Azadirachtine 5%EC (16ml/16L).

pests.name: White grub
pests.impact: Larvae damage the roots and stem parts of potato plants. After tubers form, they eat the tubers, leading to reduced potato yield and loss of market value. Significant damage occurs under dry weather conditions.
pests.solution: Deep plowing exposes larvae and pupae to the soil surface, where they are destroyed by sunlight and predatory birds. Use light traps to reduce adult beetle populations, as they are sensitive to light. Hand-pick and destroy larvae. Practice crop rotation. Maintain field sanitation. Use well-prepared organic fertilizers in recommended quantities.

pests.name: Snails and Slugs
pests.impact: They eat young leaves and shoots, hindering crop growth. Damage is more prevalent during periods of high rainfall.
pests.solution: Improve field sanitation. Maintain good weed control. Remove potential hiding places from the field during the day. Inspect the field at night, hand-collect, and destroy them. Place bait traps throughout the field. Apply recommended molluscicides: Metaldehyde 3% RB (10-40kg/ha), Metaldehyde 4% RB (10-40kg/ha), Metaldehyde 5% RB (10-40kg/ha).

diseases.name: Late Blight
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes rapid blight of leaves, stems, and tubers.
diseases.solution: Timely planting, cultivate resistant varieties, use healthy seed potatoes, practice crop rotation with non-Solanaceae crops, fallow the cultivation land for about 3 years, use recommended fungicides (Mancozeb, Maneb, Metalxyl, Fluazinam).

diseases.name: Early Blight
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes dark, concentric spots on leaves.
diseases.solution: Practice crop rotation, use recommended fungicides (Mancozeb, Maneb).

diseases.name: Potato Powdery Scab
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes powdery lesions on tubers.
diseases.solution: Use disease-free, clean seed potatoes for cultivation, fallow the cultivation land for a minimum of 3 years.

diseases.name: Black Scurf
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes black sclerotia on tubers and canker on stems.
diseases.solution: Use disease-free, clean seed potatoes for cultivation, practice crop rotation.

diseases.name: Common Scab
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes corky lesions on tubers.
diseases.solution: Use disease-free, clean seed potatoes for cultivation, maintain soil pH between 5.0-5.2, practice crop rotation, improve soil drainage.

diseases.name: Bacterial Wilt
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes wilting of plants.
diseases.solution: Use disease-free, clean seed potatoes for cultivation, fallow the cultivation land for a minimum of 3 years, improve soil drainage.

diseases.name: Dry Rot
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes dry, sunken lesions on tubers.
diseases.solution: Practice crop rotation, use disease-free, clean seed potatoes for cultivation.

diseases.name: Viral Diseases
diseases.impact: Not explicitly detailed, but generally causes various symptoms like mosaic, curling, and stunting.
diseases.solution: Control insect vectors of viral diseases (whitefly, aphid), regularly inspect the crop and remove virus-infected plants from the field.
